This moves beyond static weighting or sequential training to a fully integrated, adaptive system.

proposals.title: ChartSpanLM: Hierarchical Span Prediction for Structured Language Model Fine-tuning
proposals.motivation: Current language models, while powerful, often treat text as a flat sequence, struggling with tasks requiring explicit recognition of nested or overlapping structures like semantic roles or nested entities. This limitation hinders their ability to capture complex compositional semantics and perform precise, structured prediction tasks common in information extraction and generation. A method that jointly models hierarchical span structure within the flexible framework of pre-trained LMs is a critical open problem.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a chart-based neural structured model, integrated into the fine-tuning of a language model, can enable accurate and globally coherent prediction of hierarchically nested spans, outperforming flat or pipeline approaches on structured prediction tasks.
proposals.method: We will adapt the chart-based paradigm from syntactic parsing for general span prediction. A transformer encoder will process the input, and a lightweight scoring head will compute a real-valued score for every possible contiguous span (start, end, label). For inference, we will use a CKY-style dynamic programming algorithm to find the globally optimal set of non-crossing, potentially nested spans. For generation tasks like masked span infilling, the chart scores will guide a constrained decoding process, ensuring the generated content respects the predicted span boundaries and labels.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a unified, trainable framework for hierarchical span prediction that can be directly applied to diverse NLP tasks without task-specific architectures. It will provide a new evaluation benchmark for structured LM capabilities and a dataset for training models to reason about text span composition. The method aims to improve performance on nested NER, semantic role labeling, and coherent long-form generation.
proposals.novelty: While chart parsers exist for syntax, applying this structured prediction framework generically to all span types (entities, roles, masked content) within a modern neural LM fine-tuning setup is novel. It moves beyond flat BIO tagging or cascade models to a single, globally normalized joint model of span hierarchy.

proposals.title: Self-Supervised Span Induction for Linguistically-Informed Masked Language Modeling
proposals.motivation: Current masked language models (MLMs) like BERT rely on random or heuristic masking strategies, which ignore the linguistic coherence of text spans. While methods like SpanBERT and ERNIE show benefits from masking contiguous spans, they depend on fixed-length heuristics or external parsers, limiting their adaptability across languages and domains. A self-supervised method to induce coherent, data-driven span priors would make span masking more robust and universally applicable, particularly for low-resource and multilingual settings.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating a learnable span inducer directly into the MLM pre-training objective, where span boundaries are optimized to align with points of high predictive uncertainty in a concurrently trained language model, will yield a dynamic masking strategy that improves the model's linguistic understanding and downstream task performance without external annotations.
proposals.method: We propose a dual-model architecture where a primary Transformer encoder is trained for masked span reconstruction. A lightweight, auxiliary span-inducing network processes the same input sequence and outputs a probability distribution over potential span boundaries. This network is trained with an auxiliary loss that maximizes the reconstruction difficulty for the primary model, encouraging boundaries at points of high uncertainty as predicted by a small, concurrently trained neural language model head. The training objective combines the standard span reconstruction loss with this boundary-coherence loss, enabling joint optimization of segmentation and language modeling.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a fully self-supervised framework for learning linguistically coherent span masks, eliminating dependency on parsers. We will provide a new evaluation benchmark for assessing the linguistic quality of induced spans across multiple languages. The resulting models are expected to show improved performance on span-level tasks like question answering and coreference resolution, especially in multilingual and domain-shift scenarios.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that uses static heuristics or external tools, our method learns to segment for masking dynamically as part of pre-training. The key innovation is using the uncertainty of a concurrently trained model as a learning signal for span boundary induction, creating a closed-loop, self-supervised system.

proposals.title: Pareto-Optimal Pre-training: Learning Robust Representations via Multi-Objective Task Ensembles
proposals.motivation: Current pre-training objectives like masked language modeling (MLM) excel at local token-level understanding but can underperform on tasks requiring longer-range coherence. While combining objectives (e.g., MLM with span prediction) is common, they are typically collapsed into a single weighted loss, which may lead to suboptimal trade-offs and representation conflicts. A principled framework is needed to explicitly manage competing objectives during pre-training to yield more robust and generalizable language models.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicitly modeling pre-training as a multi-objective optimization problem and maintaining a Pareto-optimal ensemble of models for complementary tasks (e.g., MLM and span prediction) will produce a final model—via selection or distillation—with superior robustness and generalization across diverse downstream evaluations compared to single-objective or scalarized multi-task training.
proposals.method: We will adapt evolutionary multi-objective optimization algorithms (e.g., NSGA-II) to neural network pre-training. A population of models will be trained, with each model's fitness defined by its performance on two distinct self-supervised objectives (e.g., MLM loss and span boundary prediction accuracy). The algorithm will maintain a diverse Pareto front of models representing optimal trade-offs. The final model will be produced by either selecting the front's knee point or by distilling the ensemble's knowledge into a single transformer via attention and hidden state matching.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel pre-training paradigm grounded in multi-objective optimization, providing a systematic way to balance competing self-supervised signals. It will yield a new state-of-the-art model and a public ensemble, accompanied by a comprehensive analysis of how different Pareto-optimal solutions specialize on distinct linguistic phenomena (e.g., syntax vs. discourse).
proposals.novelty: While multi-task learning is standard, applying Pareto optimization to maintain an explicit *ensemble* of optimally trade-offed models during pre-training, rather than a single converged point, is a novel approach inspired by evolutionary algorithms but not yet explored in large-scale language model training.

proposals.title: EdgeSpanRank: Co-designing Span-Based Pre-training and On-Device Learning-to-Rank for Multi-Document Evidence Selection
proposals.motivation: The need for private, low-latency information retrieval on edge devices is growing, but current neural rankers are too large and lack the nuanced, context-aware understanding required for evidence selection across documents. Pre-2026, models like SpanBERT excel at span-level understanding but are not optimized for ranking, while efficient rankers often rely on coarse sentence embeddings. Bridging this gap is critical for applications like on-device fact-checking and legal analysis, which demand both deep linguistic reasoning and operational efficiency without cloud dependency.
proposals.hypothesis: A compact transformer model, co-designed through span-masking pre-training and direct fine-tuning for listwise ranking, can achieve state-of-the-art evidence selection accuracy on edge hardware by generating representations that are intrinsically optimized for comparative relevance judgments over textual spans.
proposals.method: We will distill a SpanBERT-base architecture into a 6-layer transformer, preserving its span masking objective during continued pre-training on a corpus of multi-document evidence (e.g., FEVER, legal case summaries). For fine-tuning, we will attach a lightweight listwise ranking head (e.g., a ListNet variant) and train end-to-end on sentence-level relevance labels, using techniques like gradient checkpointing and quantization-aware training. Evaluation will be conducted on multilingual datasets (e.g., XOR-TyDi QA) to measure ranking performance (nDCG, MRR) versus inference latency and memory footprint on mobile CPUs.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, open-source model that sets a new benchmark for accuracy-efficiency trade-offs in on-device evidence ranking. It will also provide a methodological framework for co-designing pre-training objectives with downstream ranking tasks, advancing the field of efficient NLP. The resulting model enables new privacy-preserving applications in mobile information retrieval.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that either adapts large retrievers or uses generic sentence encoders, this proposal uniquely integrates span-aware representation learning directly with a learning-to-rank objective, creating a single model whose pre-training is explicitly aligned with the nuances of cross-document evidence comparison under strict resource constraints.

proposals.title: Multi-Resolution Pre-training: Scaling Foundational Models via Coarse-to-Fine Language Acquisition
proposals.motivation: Scaling language models requires immense compute and data, but current methods treat all data uniformly, potentially wasting resources on low-quality text. A more efficient paradigm is needed to leverage the vast scale of raw web data without sacrificing the nuanced understanding gained from high-quality corpora. This work investigates whether staged, resolution-adapted training can improve scaling efficiency and final model performance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that initializing a model with coarse, large-scale patterns from a massive, low-fidelity corpus will create a better foundation for subsequent fine-grained, span-based pre-training on a curated dataset, leading to more sample-efficient scaling and superior downstream task performance compared to single-stage training on the high-quality data alone.
proposals.method: We will first pre-train a standard decoder-only Transformer (e.g., OLMo architecture) on a multi-trillion token, low-fidelity corpus using next-token prediction. This 'coarse' model establishes scaling laws and foundational knowledge. We then use its weights to initialize a second model for a 'fine' pre-training stage on a high-quality, curated dataset (e.g., Dolma, FineWeb) using the SpanBERT objective, which masks contiguous random spans to train robust contextual representations. We will systematically vary the ratio of coarse-to-fine compute and data to map the scaling efficiency frontier.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, efficient pre-training recipe and a detailed analysis of multi-resolution scaling laws for language models. It will contribute a new perspective on leveraging data heterogeneity, potentially reducing the compute cost of reaching a given performance level. The final models and code will be released to advance open research in efficient training.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly adapting the multi-resolution simulation paradigm from computational cosmology to LLM pre-training, formally treating data quality as a 'resolution' parameter. This structured, staged approach to leveraging heterogeneous data scales is distinct from prior mixed-quality training or continual pre-training methods.

proposals.title: Scaling SpanBERT: Investigating the Limits of Span Masking and Corpus Size on Language Model Performance
proposals.motivation: Scaling laws for autoregressive models like GPT-3 are established, but the scaling behavior of span-masked models like SpanBERT is less understood. SpanBERT's objective is more sample-efficient for tasks requiring span-level understanding, but it is unclear if its performance continues to improve with orders of magnitude more data. This research is critical to determine the most data-efficient and performant pre-training paradigms for future models.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that SpanBERT's performance, particularly on tasks requiring reasoning about contiguous text spans, will continue to improve predictably with increased corpus size, but that the scaling exponent and saturation points will differ significantly from those of autoregressive models.
proposals.method: We will implement a large-scale, multilingual pre-training pipeline based on the SpanBERT architecture and objective (masking random contiguous spans). We will systematically pre-train models on corpora scaled from 100B to 10T+ tokens, using the domain discovery methods from 'Scaling Expert Language Models' to ensure data diversity. We will rigorously evaluate scaling trends on a suite of tasks requiring span-level understanding (e.g., coreference resolution, question answering, extractive summarization) and general language understanding (GLUE, SuperGLUE).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce new scaling laws for span-masked language models, providing a data-driven foundation for future model development. It will also yield a set of powerful, publicly released SpanBERT models pre-trained on unprecedented data volumes, along with a detailed analysis of how scaling affects specific linguistic capabilities.
proposals.novelty: While scaling autoregressive models is well-studied, this is the first systematic investigation of extreme-scale pre-training for non-autoregressive, span-masked objectives. It uniquely combines the data efficiency of SpanBERT with the scale of modern corpora to test the fundamental limits of this pre-training paradigm.

proposals.title: Dual-Dist: Modeling Ambiguity in Span Boundary Prediction with Probabilistic Distributions
proposals.motivation: Current span prediction models in NLP, such as those for entity recognition or question answering, typically output a single, deterministic span. This fails to capture the inherent ambiguity of natural language, where multiple span interpretations can be semantically valid given a context. A model that quantifies this ambiguity through a full probability distribution over boundaries would provide a more robust and informative representation, crucial for downstream reasoning and uncertainty-aware systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Training a model to output a joint probability distribution over all possible start and end boundaries, conditioned on masked content and context, will allow it to rank multiple plausible interpretations and robustly recover ground-truth spans as high-likelihood candidates.
proposals.method: We will adapt a transformer-based encoder (e.g., T5 or a multilingual BART variant) to perform seq2seq generation of a structured output representing a probability matrix. For a sequence of length N, the model will be trained to output a normalized N x N matrix where each cell (i,j) represents the likelihood of a span from token i to j. Training will use a maximum likelihood objective, comparing the predicted distribution against a smoothed target derived from annotated spans. We will evaluate on multilingual datasets with known structural ambiguities, such as nested NER or scope resolution.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new probabilistic framework for span prediction that explicitly models ambiguity, moving beyond single-point estimates. It will provide a novel evaluation paradigm for assessing model sensitivity to linguistic ambiguity and deliver a more informative representation for tasks requiring uncertainty quantification.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that outputs a single span or a set of discrete candidates, our method directly learns a full, normalized joint distribution over all possible boundaries, treating span prediction as a structured density estimation problem over the lattice of indices.

proposals.title: DUAL: Decoding Unit Boundaries via Asymmetric Language Modeling
proposals.motivation: Current language models (LMs) excel at predicting content given boundaries (e.g., masked token prediction) but struggle with the inverse: predicting structural boundaries from content. This capability is crucial for tasks like semantic segmentation, information extraction, and improving model interpretability by understanding how models internally segment sequences. Grounded in pre-2026 work on context-aware training and vocabulary learning, this addresses a core gap in modeling the relationship between content and structure.
proposals.hypothesis: A model trained to predict span boundaries (start and end positions) given only the masked content and its bidirectional context will learn richer, more compositional representations of linguistic units than standard masked language modeling, leading to improved performance on structure-sensitive downstream tasks.
proposals.method: We will train a Transformer-based encoder model using a novel dual masking objective. For a given text, we will randomly sample contiguous spans, replace them with a single [MASK] token, and task the model with regressing the original span's start and end positions. The architecture will use a specialized output head for boundary prediction. We will evaluate on multilingual datasets to test cross-lingual generalization of the learned segmentation principles, benchmarking against baselines on tasks like chunking, named entity recognition, and probing for hierarchical structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce and validate a new pre-training objective that explicitly models the mapping from content to structure. It will provide a novel diagnostic tool for analyzing how LMs segment information and could lead to more data-efficient models for sequence labeling tasks. The resulting models and code will be released as a public resource.
proposals.novelty: While masked language modeling predicts content within known boundaries, DUAL inverts this to predict boundaries from content, a formally distinct and understudied problem. This asymmetric formulation is non-obvious compared to standard span-boundary or pointer-network approaches, focusing the model's capacity on structural inference from contextual cues alone.

proposals.title: Structure-Informed Multi-Task Masking for Pre-Training Foundational Models on Biological Sequences
proposals.motivation: Pre-training on non-text sequential data like proteins and DNA using standard NLP masking strategies (e.g., BERT's random token or span masking) fails to capture the critical hierarchical and functional dependencies inherent in these domains. This limits the quality and sample efficiency of the resulting foundational models for downstream scientific tasks. A pre-training objective that explicitly incorporates domain-specific structural priors is needed to learn richer, more generalizable representations.
proposals.hypothesis: A multi-granular, structure-aware masking strategy, combined with auxiliary prediction tasks derived from domain knowledge, will produce pre-trained models that learn more robust, multi-scale representations of biological sequences, leading to superior performance on diverse downstream tasks compared to models trained with standard masking objectives.
proposals.method: We will develop a dynamic masking algorithm that, for a given sequence (e.g., a protein), selects masks at multiple granularities—from single residues to functional motifs—guided by predicted or known structural annotations (e.g., secondary structure segments, solvent accessibility). The core Transformer-based encoder will be trained with a multi-task objective: reconstructing the masked tokens and simultaneously predicting auxiliary labels (e.g., contact maps, local structural features) for the masked regions. This forces the model to integrate local context with long-range, structure-defining interactions during pre-training.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a principled, general framework for injecting structural domain knowledge into the pre-training process for biological sequences. It will deliver new, open-sourced pre-trained models for proteins and DNA that achieve state-of-the-art results on function prediction and engineering benchmarks. The methodology will provide a blueprint for applying similar structure-aware pre-training to other non-text domains like time-series.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work that applies text-style masking or adds structural prediction as a separate fine-tuning stage, our method dynamically integrates multi-scale structural prediction as an integral, concurrent component of the pre-training objective itself, creating a direct feedback loop between token reconstruction and structural consistency.

proposals.title: Cross-Modal Scaling Laws: Emergent Sequence Modeling in Text, Biology, and Time-Series via Unified Span Pre-training
proposals.motivation: Recent scaling of language models has revealed emergent capabilities like in-context learning, but it is unknown if these phenomena are unique to natural language or reflect more general principles of sequence modeling. A systematic investigation across structurally similar but semantically distinct modalities—text, biological sequences, and time-series—is required to determine if intelligence-like behaviors are a function of scale, data, or domain-specific architecture. This work directly addresses a core question in complex systems science applied to AI: what computational primitives are universal?
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single transformer model, pre-trained with a span masking objective on massive, multi-modal sequence corpora, will exhibit cross-modal scaling laws and that key emergent capabilities (e.g., in-context learning, compositional reasoning) will transfer across domains once a critical scale of data and parameters is reached.
proposals.method: We will pre-train a unified, dense transformer architecture using a span masking objective (e.g., T5-style) on three curated, unprecedented-scale corpora: multilingual web text, assembled genomic/protein sequences, and structured multivariate time-series. We will scale model size and data volume in a controlled, joint fashion across modalities, monitoring performance on established and newly introduced evaluation suites for each domain. The core analysis will involve fitting and comparing scaling laws per modality and searching for inflection points where capabilities emerge or transfer, using techniques from unsupervised domain discovery to analyze learned representations.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first empirical cross-modal scaling laws, a massive multi-modal pre-trained model, and a rigorous evaluation framework for universal sequence modeling. It will provide concrete evidence for or against the hypothesis of shared computational principles for intelligence across symbolic, biological, and physical systems.
proposals.novelty: While span masking and multi-modal training exist, the novelty lies in using scale as the primary controlled experimental variable across diverse non-text modalities to explicitly test for transfer of emergent capabilities, moving beyond simple multi-task learning to a fundamental inquiry into universality.

proposals.title: Unified Task and Domain Adapters: A Modular Framework for Efficient Multi-Task and Transfer Learning
proposals.motivation: Current multi-task and transfer learning approaches often rely on full fine-tuning or ad-hoc adapter modules, which are inefficient and lack a unified abstraction for managing interference and synergy between tasks and domains. This complicates systematic experimentation and scaling, as seen in the challenges of scaling expert language models and analyzing their emergent behaviors. A principled framework is needed to better understand and control how knowledge transfers across linguistic tasks and domains.
proposals.hypothesis: A modular framework based on a shared set of composable, sparsely activated adapters, discovered via unsupervised domain/task clustering, will enable more parameter-efficient multi-task learning and provide clearer abstractions for analyzing transfer dynamics and interference.
proposals.method: We will extend the Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) paradigm, drawing from 'Scaling Expert Language Models with Unsupervised Domain Discovery', to learn a shared vocabulary of lightweight adapter modules. An unsupervised task/domain discovery algorithm will cluster training data to define expert specializations. A novel gating network, conditioned on both task identifier and input context, will dynamically compose these adapters. We will evaluate this framework on a multilingual, multi-task benchmark (e.g., CrossFit or a custom suite) against baselines like full fine-tuning, standard adapters, and multi-task dense models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical, open-source framework for easier design of multi-task and transfer experiments. It will contribute new insights into the conditions for positive and negative transfer by analyzing adapter activation patterns, advancing the complex systems science of language models. The method aims to improve performance and data efficiency in low-resource transfer scenarios.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior adapter-based methods, our approach unifies task and domain transfer under a single, discoverable adapter vocabulary with context-aware composition, moving beyond static task-to-expert assignments. This provides a novel abstraction for probing model behavior across the task-domain spectrum.

proposals.title: Span-Aware Cross-Document NSP: Learning to Filter and Fuse Noisy Evidence for Robust Distant Supervision
proposals.motivation: Distant supervision is a powerful paradigm for scaling training data, but it introduces significant noise from contradictory or irrelevant evidence sentences. Standard pre-training objectives like Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) operate on clean, contiguous text, failing to teach models to reason about noisy, cross-document evidence. This limits the robustness of models trained on distantly supervised data for tasks like open-domain QA and fact verification.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing the standard NSP objective with a cross-document, span-aware variant that requires predicting fine-grained semantic relationships (e.g., entailment, contradiction, neutral) between evidence spans will train models to inherently filter noise and resolve conflicts, leading to more robust representations for distantly supervised tasks.
proposals.method: We will construct training instances from large-scale, multilingual corpora (e.g., Wikipedia, news archives) by linking sentences about the same entity/event across different documents. The model architecture will extend a standard transformer encoder (e.g., OLMo-style) with a span-pooling layer and a multi-layer classifier head. Instead of a binary NSP label, the objective will be a multi-class prediction over semantic relations between a target span in the context document and a candidate sentence from a linked document. Training will use a curriculum, starting with cleaner data and progressively introducing noisier, distantly gathered pairs.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel pre-training objective that directly addresses the noise problem in distant supervision. It will yield models with improved robustness for evidence integration and a new benchmark for evaluating cross-document reasoning under noise. The method provides a pathway to leverage noisier, web-scale data more effectively.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard NSP or recent contrastive learning methods, this approach explicitly trains models to perform fine-grained, span-level semantic reasoning between non-contiguous, potentially contradictory text snippets from different documents, fusing distant supervision with structured prediction.

proposals.title: Fact-Atomic Decomposition and Composition for Verifiable Open-Ended Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) struggle with verifiable, multi-fact synthesis, often producing coherent but ungrounded or contradictory narratives. This is a critical bottleneck for applications requiring trustworthy long-form generation from multiple sources, such as summarizing scientific literature or compiling technical reports. Existing benchmarks and training methods lack a structured framework to decompose information needs into atomic, verifiable units and then explicitly compose them, limiting our ability to train and evaluate models rigorously on this core capability.
proposals.hypothesis: By training models to explicitly decompose an information need into a set of atomic, verifiable fact units and a composition graph, and then generate a narrative conditioned on this structured representation, we can significantly improve the factual accuracy, logical coherence, and verifiability of open-ended synthesis compared to end-to-end generation.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage model architecture. First, a decomposition module, inspired by unsupervised domain discovery methods, will generate a set of atomic fact queries and a directed acyclic graph specifying their logical/temporal relationships. Second, a composition module will retrieve or generate evidence for each atomic fact and then generate a final narrative, strictly conditioned on the verified facts and the graph structure. We will create a new multilingual evaluation dataset where answers are annotated with fact-unit decompositions and composition graphs, enabling granular evaluation of both fact correctness and narrative flow.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel training and evaluation framework for synthesis that bridges atomic fact verification and high-level coherence, 2) a new dataset enabling fine-grained analysis of model errors in multi-step reasoning, and 3) a model that demonstrates improved performance on verifiable synthesis tasks with reduced hallucination, advancing towards more reliable and interpretable LLM behavior.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has focused on fact verification or end-to-end generation, our approach uniquely integrates unsupervised structure discovery (for graph induction) with constrained generation, making the implicit compositional reasoning of LLMs explicit, trainable, and evaluable at multiple granularities.

proposals.title: Joint Optimization of Retrieval and Synthesis for Open-Domain Multi-Source Answer Generation
proposals.motivation: Current open-domain QA systems typically treat retrieval and generation as separate stages, where a retriever fetches evidence and a reader generates an answer. This pipeline approach is suboptimal for complex questions requiring synthesis across multiple documents, as the retriever is not optimized for the downstream need to integrate and reason over disparate pieces of information. This limits the ability to produce coherent, non-extractive answers that truly fuse knowledge from a dynamic context.
proposals.hypothesis: A model jointly trained to retrieve documents for the express purpose of multi-document synthesis will generate more accurate and coherent answers to complex open-domain questions than systems with decoupled retrieval and generation components.
proposals.method: We will build upon dense passage retrieval (DPR) and encoder-decoder architectures (e.g., T5, BART). The core innovation is a single model trained end-to-end with a multi-task objective: it must learn to retrieve a small set of relevant documents (via a differentiable maximum inner product search approximation) and then generate an answer conditioned on the full, retrieved set. The training will use a novel dataset constructed from existing multi-hop QA benchmarks (e.g., HotpotQA, 2WikiMultihop) and synthetically augmented examples requiring cross-document fusion. The model's vocabulary and contextual representations will be explicitly probed to understand the fusion process.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a new architecture and training paradigm for integrated retrieval-and-synthesis, moving beyond pipeline systems. It will provide a rigorous evaluation framework for measuring synthesis quality in generative QA, and yield insights into how models can learn to dynamically manage context for complex reasoning tasks.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard retrieve-then-read pipelines or models with fixed evidence, our method jointly optimizes retrieval for the specific goal of generative synthesis, allowing the retriever's behavior to be directly shaped by the generator's need to fuse information across a variable number of sources.

proposals.title: MegaSpan: Scaling Span-Level Pre-Training for Coherent Multi-Document Evidence Synthesis
proposals.motivation: Current large language models (LLMs) for question answering often rely on retrieved contexts that are limited in size, leading to fragmented or contradictory answers when evidence is spread across many documents. Existing models lack explicit architectural mechanisms for fine-grained, cross-document coreference and consistency resolution at the entity and proposition level, which is critical for complex tasks like scientific literature review or investigative journalism. A model capable of internalizing and synthesizing evidence from a massive corpus of documents in a single forward pass would represent a significant advance in grounded reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: Massively scaling up a span-level masked language model (like SpanBERT), while preserving its span selection objective and explicitly training it on multi-document corpora, will enable the emergent capability to perform implicit cross-document coreference and contradiction resolution, leading to more coherent and precisely grounded multi-document synthesis.
proposals.method: We will architect and pre-train a transformer model at the scale of hundreds of billions of parameters, using a novel multi-document span masking objective. The training data will consist of curated, interlinked document clusters (e.g., Wikipedia article networks, news threads, scientific paper citations). The objective will mask contiguous spans within one document and require the model to predict them using context from the entire cluster. We will enhance the architecture with a global attention mechanism over a large, fixed-size document cache (e.g., 128+ documents) and introduce a specialized evaluation dataset to measure cross-document coreference and synthesis performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new state-of-the-art model for evidence synthesis that generates answers directly grounded in precise source spans across a large corpus. It will advance our understanding of how scale and specialized pre-training objectives affect a model's ability to manage and reconcile information from multiple sources internally, moving beyond retrieval-augmented generation paradigms.
proposals.novelty: While scaling language models is common, scaling a *span-level* model specifically for multi-document understanding is unexplored. This approach uniquely combines the fine-grained grounding of span-based models with the massive context capacity of modern LLMs to solve the multi-document synthesis problem end-to-end, rather than through chained retrieval steps.

proposals.title: The Emergence of In-Context Robustness: Probing Random Label Sensitivity in Tiny Language Models
proposals.motivation: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit a surprising capacity for in-context learning (ICL), including robustness to random or flipped labels in demonstrations. This behavior challenges classical supervised learning and suggests LLMs rely heavily on contextual patterns. However, it is unknown whether this robustness is an emergent property of scale or a fundamental characteristic of the transformer architecture's contextual processing. Studying this in tiny models isolates architectural and training dynamics from sheer scale.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that very small language models (e.g., <10M parameters), when trained on sufficiently diverse data, will exhibit similar robustness to random in-context labels as large models, indicating that this capability is not purely scale-dependent but linked to specific training and architectural factors.
proposals.method: We will train a suite of transformer-based language models (1M to 100M parameters) from scratch on the C4 corpus. We will evaluate ICL using a controlled, multilingual evaluation suite of classification tasks (e.g., from SuperGLUE format). We will systematically vary the proportion and placement of random/flipped labels in the in-context demonstrations and measure performance degradation relative to a clean-context baseline. Analysis will include probing internal representations and attention patterns to compare mechanisms between small and large model behaviors.
proposals.contribution: This work will delineate the scale requirements for emergent ICL robustness, contributing to the science of model scaling. It will produce a public dataset of tiny model ICL evaluations and could inform more efficient model training strategies by identifying minimal sufficient conditions for contextual adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While random label robustness is known in large models, systematically testing its limits in tiny, custom-trained models is unexplored. This bridges the 'Scaling Expert' and 'Complex Systems Science' themes by treating scale as an experimental variable to understand foundational model behaviors.

ideas.title: Self-Supervised Induction of Coherent Span Priors
ideas.description: This approach replaces reliance on external parsers with a learned, self-supervised span inducer integrated into the masking pre-training objective. The model is trained to simultaneously segment sequences into coherent spans and reconstruct the masked content from those spans, using an auxiliary loss that encourages span boundaries to align with points of high uncertainty in a concurrently trained neural language model. This results in a dynamic, data-driven prior for span masking that adapts to the domain and language of the training corpus. The system thus gains the benefits of linguistically informed masking without the fragility of pre-defined annotations.

ideas.title: Scale up pre-training with much larger datasets
ideas.description: Train BART on orders-of-magnitude more text data to explore scaling laws for denoising pre-training.
ideas.sourceTitle: BART: Denoising Sequence-to-Sequence Pre-training for Natural Language Generation, Translation, and Comprehension

ideas.title: Massive-Scale Span Pre-training for Universal Sequence Modeling
ideas.description: This research direction proposes pre-training a single, unified transformer architecture using the span masking objective on massive, multi-modal corpora encompassing text, biological sequences (DNA, proteins), and structured time-series data, each at unprecedented scale. By systematically scaling the model and data across these diverse but structurally similar modalities simultaneously, the project aims to identify cross-modal scaling laws and determine if emergent capabilities in one domain (e.g., in-context learning in text) transfer or manifest in others. The core novelty is using scale as a controlled variable to test whether a universal sequence modeling framework emerges, potentially revealing shared computational principles and data requirements for intelligence across symbolic, biological, and physical systems.

ideas.title: Improve training stability for large models
ideas.description: Develop better techniques to stabilize training of models at the scale of 175B parameters and beyond.
ideas.sourceTitle: OPT: Open Pre-trained Transformer Language Models

ideas.title: Pre-train on Massive Corpora Using a Staged, Multi-Resolution Approach
ideas.description: This idea adapts the multi-scale 'zoom-in' simulation technique from cosmology to the challenge of pre-training on orders of magnitude more text data. In cosmology, large-scale, low-resolution simulations establish the cosmic context, which then guides detailed, high-resolution simulations of specific regions. Analogously, we propose a staged pre-training regimen: first, train a model on a vast, possibly lower-fidelity corpus (e.g., raw web text with simple tokenization) to learn scaling laws and capture extensive but coarse patterns. This model then serves as the initialization for a second, focused pre-training phase using the SpanBERT method on a high-quality, curated dataset. This adapted approach tests scaling laws for foundational knowledge acquisition while ensuring the final model benefits from the nuanced, structured learning of span-based objectives, potentially leading to more efficient and effective scaling.

ideas.title: Challenge single-dataset finetuning for robustness
ideas.description: Finetune models on mixtures of diverse instruction datasets simultaneously to improve generalization and reduce dataset bias.
ideas.sourceTitle: QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs

ideas.title: Multi-Granular, Structure-Aware Masking for Pre-Training on Non-Text Sequential Data
ideas.description: This redesigned approach moves beyond simple contiguous span masking by implementing a dynamic, multi-granular corruption strategy tailored to the inherent structure of DNA, protein, or time-series data. The pre-training objective combines the reconstruction of masked tokens with auxiliary prediction tasks that force the model to learn multi-scale and long-range dependencies critical to the domain. For example, when masking a region of a protein sequence, the model might also be tasked with predicting its predicted solvent accessibility or its contact with another unmasked region, integrating structural biology priors. This results in a more robust foundational model that captures the hierarchical and relational patterns essential for downstream tasks in non-text domains.

ideas.title: Use adversarial objectives from domain adaptation

ideas.title: Adaptive Multi-Objective Pre-training with Gradient Conflict Resolution
ideas.description: This redesigned system integrates span boundary prediction, sentence order prediction, and potentially other tasks within a unified framework that dynamically manages their interaction. It employs gradient conflict resolution techniques to align the updates from different objectives, preventing interference. Furthermore, it uses a meta-learning-inspired controller to adaptively weight each objective's contribution for every batch based on the model's current proficiency and the inherent difficulty of the sample. This results in more stable, efficient training and a model with stronger, more harmoniously integrated linguistic capabilities.

ideas.title: Multi-Objective Pre-training via Complementary Task Ensembles
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology and multi-objective optimization, this idea adapts the concept of Pareto optimization to neural network pre-training. In evolutionary algorithms, multiple competing objectives are balanced by maintaining a diverse Pareto front of solutions rather than collapsing them into a single weighted sum. Adapted to the original idea of combining span prediction with masked language modeling, this suggests implementing a multi-objective pre-training framework that explicitly maintains an ensemble of models representing different optimal trade-offs between the two self-supervised tasks. The final model would either be selected from this Pareto-optimal set or would distill knowledge from the ensemble, potentially yielding more robust representations that better capture both local token-level and longer-range span-level linguistic patterns.

ideas.title: Invert the noising direction for data augmentation
ideas.description: Use the trained BART model to generate realistic noisy text from clean text for data augmentation.
ideas.sourceTitle: BART: Denoising Sequence-to-Sequence Pre-training for Natural Language Generation, Translation, and Comprehension

ideas.title: EdgeSpanRank: On-Device Span-Based Learning-to-Rank for Multi-Document Evidence Selection
ideas.description: This research creates a compact, span-aware transformer model specifically designed to perform learning-to-rank for evidence sentences across multiple documents directly on mobile or edge hardware. The model leverages a distilled SpanBERT architecture to generate rich, span-based representations of candidate sentences, which are then scored and ranked using a lightweight listwise or pairwise ranking head optimized for low latency. This enables applications like on-device fact-checking or legal document analysis to retrieve the most relevant evidence from a local corpus without cloud dependency, balancing deep linguistic understanding with the efficiency constraints of edge computing. The core innovation is the co-design of span masking pre-training and ranking fine-tuning to produce a single model that excels at both understanding textual continuity and performing comparative relevance judgments under strict resource limits.

ideas.title: Cross-Document Span-Aware NSP for Robust Distant Supervision
ideas.description: This research fuses the critical examination of noisy distant supervision with a novel span-aware Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) variant. The core idea is to train models using distantly gathered evidence documents, where the 'next sentence' is not a clean contiguous text, but a potentially noisy or contradictory candidate sentence from a different document that discusses the same entity or event. The model must perform span-aware reasoning to predict not just a binary relationship, but the degree of semantic alignment or contradiction between spans in the context and the candidate, thereby learning to filter noise and resolve conflicts inherent in distantly supervised data.

ideas.title: Explore more diverse or structured noising functions

ideas.title: Dual-Dist: Probabilistic Span Boundary Distribution from Masked Content
ideas.description: This system trains a model to predict a full probability distribution over all possible span boundaries conditioned on the masked content and its surrounding context. Instead of a single output, the model assigns a likelihood score to every candidate span, explicitly handling the inherent ambiguity of natural language. This allows the system to identify and rank multiple semantically valid interpretations, providing a more robust and informative representation of the content-boundary relationship. The approach is evaluated on its ability to recover ground-truth boundaries as high-probability candidates and to capture known linguistic ambiguities.

ideas.title: Apply SpanBERT to span-level tasks using a unified multi-head adapter framework
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from modular robotics, where a core robot uses interchangeable end-effectors for different tasks, this idea proposes a unified framework for applying SpanBERT to diverse span-level tasks. The analogous solution involves equipping a versatile core with specialized, swappable modules. Adapted to NLP, the solution is to keep a pre-trained SpanBERT model frozen as a core encoder of span representations and develop a set of small, task-specific 'adapter heads' for tasks like semantic role labeling, named entity recognition, and coreference resolution. This modular approach allows for efficient multi-task learning, reduces catastrophic forgetting, and enables rapid prototyping and testing on new span-level tasks by simply designing a new lightweight head, rather than fine-tuning the entire large model from scratch.

ideas.title: Open-Domain Generative Answer Synthesis
ideas.description: This research direction investigates systems that answer complex, open-domain questions requiring synthesis from multiple sources without pre-retrieved evidence. The system must first retrieve relevant documents from a large corpus, then analyze and integrate information across them to construct a coherent answer that is not a direct extract from any single source. This challenges models to perform joint optimization of retrieval for synthesis and generation grounded in a dynamic, multi-evidence context, pushing beyond the capabilities of standard QA pipelines.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Span Prediction with Chart-based Neural Structured Models
ideas.description: This approach addresses the flat segmentation limitation by predicting masked or target spans using a chart-based neural structured model. The system encodes the input sequence and then scores all possible contiguous spans, using these scores in a global inference step to select the optimal set of spans, which can be hierarchically nested. This allows for the joint modeling of span boundaries, labels, and internal content dependencies, capturing complex compositional structure. The method is particularly suited for tasks like semantic role labeling, nested named entity recognition, or generating coherent multi-token fill-ins for masked spans in text.

ideas.title: Improve multi-task and transfer learning support
ideas.description: Enhance the framework's abstractions for easier multi-task learning and transfer learning experiments.
ideas.sourceTitle: AllenNLP: A Deep Semantic Natural Language Processing Platform

ideas.title: Scaled Multi-Document Span-Level Evidence Synthesis
ideas.description: This research direction proposes training a massively scaled-up version of SpanBERT, specifically architected and pre-trained for the task of multi-document synthesis. The model's span-level masking objective and span selection capabilities are preserved and enhanced at scale, allowing it to develop a sophisticated understanding of entity and proposition consistency across documents. The scaled capacity enables the model to process and integrate information from a large set of evidence documents simultaneously, far beyond typical retrieval-augmented limits, performing cross-document coreference and contradiction resolution internally. The goal is a single model that can answer complex questions by generating a synthesized summary grounded in precise spans from across the entire provided evidence corpus.

ideas.title: Apply curriculum learning from machine learning

ideas.title: Structured Synthesis via Fact-Atomic Decomposition and Composition
ideas.description: This research addresses open-ended answer synthesis by first decomposing the information need into a set of atomic, verifiable fact units and a composition graph specifying their relationships. Models are trained to generate both the fact set and a coherent narrative structured by the graph, allowing for evaluation at the granular fact level for correctness and at the compositional level for logical flow. This approach provides a rigorous framework for training and benchmarking synthesis tasks, moving beyond single-answer extraction while maintaining verifiability and reducing hallucination.

ideas.title: Self-Paced Dynamic Span Curriculum for Language Model Pre-training
ideas.description: This approach implements an adaptive curriculum learning system for span masking that dynamically adjusts task difficulty based on the model's real-time performance. Instead of a fixed schedule, the system monitors prediction accuracy or loss for various masking configurations (defined by span length, linguistic unit, or token rarity) over a recent window of training steps. A controller then uses these competence signals to up-weight masking tasks where the model performs within a target 'challenge band,' ensuring it is consistently trained on optimally informative examples. This closed-loop, self-paced curriculum maximizes sample efficiency and learning robustness by continuously matching task difficulty to the model's evolving capabilities.

ideas.title: Adaptive Span Masking Inspired by Ecological Sampling Strategies
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea of exploring span masking strategies by drawing an analogy from ecology and environmental science. In that field, the efficiency and accuracy of estimating species abundance depend critically on the sampling strategy (e.g., random, systematic, stratified, or adaptive cluster sampling), where the size and placement of sampling plots are adapted to the underlying spatial structure of the population. Adapting this solution, we propose to design adaptive span masking strategies for language model pre-training. The core idea is to treat the text as an ecological landscape where linguistic elements (like entities or semantic concepts) have a 'spatial' structure. The masking algorithm would dynamically decide span lengths and locations based on real-time analysis of the local context—for instance, masking entire coherent phrases or clauses when they are detected, or using shorter, more frequent masks in areas of dense, fine-grained information. The research would systematically investigate whether this context-aware, adaptive approach, inspired by optimal ecological sampling, leads to better linguistic representations and improved performance on downstream tasks, particularly those requiring complex semantic understanding or long-range dependencies, compared to models trained with standard static span masking distributions.

ideas.title: Scale instruction data quality analysis to larger models
ideas.description: Investigate if the 'less is more' alignment effect holds for models significantly larger than 65B parameters.
ideas.sourceTitle: LIMA: Less Is More for Alignment

ideas.title: Curriculum Learning-to-Rank for Progressive Evidence Span Masking
ideas.description: This approach integrates learning-to-rank directly into the span masking pre-training objective. Instead of random or fixed-length spans, the model is trained to first identify and then reconstruct the most 'relevant' spans from a multi-document context, as determined by a learned ranking signal. The curriculum is applied by gradually increasing the complexity of the ranking task—starting with single-document, short, salient spans and progressing to cross-document, longer spans that require synthesizing disparate evidence. This forces the model to develop an inherent, graded understanding of evidence salience and coherence, making it more adept at complex retrieval and reasoning tasks from the pre-training stage.

ideas.title: Dual: Tools that learn to use LMs
ideas.description: Create specialized tools that learn when and how to query a large LM as a resource.

ideas.title: Achieve Robust Functional Equivalence Instead of Narrow Benchmark Performance
ideas.description: This research pursues human-machine equivalence by focusing on the robustness and generality of capabilities, not just aggregate scores. The core method involves training and evaluating models against a dynamic, automatically generated suite of tasks that test compositional reasoning, causal understanding, and skill transfer in novel contexts. A key component is an adversarial evaluation framework that systematically probes for shortcuts and brittle reasoning, forcing the development of more human-like, generalizable understanding. Success is defined by closing the gap in learning efficiency and adaptability on unseen challenges, moving beyond static benchmark leaderboards.

ideas.title: Predict masked spans via a global constraint solver adapted from computational biology
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational biology, specifically protein structure prediction, where the coordinates of atoms are not predicted independently but rather an entire molecular span's structure is found by optimizing a global scoring function that enforces physical constraints. The analogous problem in NLP is predicting the content of a masked span, where tokens have strong internal dependencies. The adapted solution is to design a model with two components: 1) A neural scoring function that, given a context and a candidate sequence for a masked span, outputs a joint plausibility score considering both internal span coherence and consistency with the surrounding context. 2) A search or optimization procedure (e.g., constrained beam search, Monte Carlo tree search, or gradient-based methods if the scorer is differentiable) to find the candidate span that maximizes this global score. This moves beyond sequential generation or pairwise token-tag dependencies to explicitly perform structured prediction over the entire span as a single, interdependent unit.

ideas.title: Challenge that format learning requires few examples
ideas.description: Systematically test the minimum data needed for reliable format learning across diverse and complex templates.
ideas.sourceTitle: LIMA: Less Is More for Alignment

ideas.title: Multi-Faceted Span-Aware Contrastive Learning for Discourse Reasoning
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond a single binary objective by integrating multiple linguistically motivated, span-aware pre-training tasks that directly target components of cross-sentence reasoning, such as causal and temporal relations. A shared contrastive learning framework aligns representations of text spans that are coherent according to these discourse principles, while pushing apart representations of spans that exhibit logical inconsistencies or discourse violations. The system dynamically mines challenging negative examples by perturbing entities, events, or discourse connectives to create semantically plausible but logically flawed alternatives. This results in a model that learns a more robust and generalizable representation of discourse structure, directly benefiting downstream tasks requiring complex cross-sentence inference.

ideas.title: Scale down: tiny models with tools

ideas.title: Scale-Dependent Span Masking: Investigating the Interaction of Model Size and Optimal Span Corruption Strategy
ideas.description: This research direction proposes to scale up SpanBERT models while simultaneously and systematically varying the span masking strategy—including distributions of span lengths, masking probabilities, and geometric parameters—across each scale. The core hypothesis is that larger models, with their increased capacity and different loss landscapes, may benefit from fundamentally different span corruption strategies compared to smaller models. By conducting this joint ablation, the work aims to identify if there is a scale-dependent optimal masking policy, thereby creating a more efficient path to scaling span-based language models. The findings could lead to new pre-training protocols where the masking strategy evolves alongside the model during scaling.

ideas.title: Syntactically-Guided Masking via Image Segmentation Priors
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computer vision, specifically medical image segmentation, where prior anatomical knowledge is used to bias models towards segmenting plausible organ boundaries. The analogous solution involves using atlases or shape models as constraints. Adapted to NLP, the method involves using syntactic parses or entity annotations to create a 'linguistic prior' that biases span masking in models like BERT towards linguistically meaningful units (e.g., noun phrases, verb phrases, named entities). Instead of random token masking, a masking probability distribution is defined over possible spans, heavily favoring those that align with the syntactic or entity structure. This trains the model's representations to better capture and predict coherent linguistic units, potentially improving performance on downstream tasks requiring structural understanding.

ideas.title: Predicting Structural Boundaries from Localized Signal in Geophysics
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from geophysical seismic inversion, where subsurface layer boundaries are predicted from localized acoustic impedance measurements and broader seismic context. The analogous problem is predicting text span boundaries given only the masked content and its sentence context. The adapted idea trains a model (e.g., a transformer) to treat the vector embedding of masked content as a localized signal property. Using this embedding and the full contextual sentence representation as input, the model directly regresses to the most probable start and end token positions, learning to 'invert' from semantic content features to structural boundary locations without explicit boundary tokens during prediction.

ideas.title: Span Curriculum Learning with Efficiency-Aware Distillation for Edge Devices
ideas.description: This system pre-trains a compact span model by first distilling general linguistic knowledge from a large teacher into a tiny student architecture. It then employs a curriculum learning strategy for span tasks, beginning with easy, short-span predictions and gradually increasing difficulty. Crucially, the training incorporates a hardware-aware efficiency loss, co-optimizing the model not just for accuracy on span objectives but for latency and energy use profiles typical of mobile CPUs, ensuring the final model is both capable and truly deployable.

ideas.title: Scale and Sparsify SpanBERT: Investigating Architectural Efficiency at Scale
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational neuroscience, where the scaling of biological neural networks follows principles of economical wiring and sparsity to maintain efficiency. The analogous problem of scaling brain size across species is solved not by uniform densification, but by increasing modularity and sparsity of long-range connections. Adapted to the research context, this suggests scaling up SpanBERT model size should investigate architectural innovations alongside pure parameter count. The research would train a series of larger SpanBERT variants where scale is achieved through sparse, efficient mechanisms like Mixture of Experts or structured sparsity, rather than solely dense layers. This approach tests whether the performance gains over BERT (particularly on span-based tasks) obey different, more favorable scaling laws when models are designed with efficiency constraints inspired by natural neural systems.

ideas.title: Dual: measure pretraining data needed for alignment

ideas.title: Multi-Task Pre-training with a Hierarchical Objective Scheduler
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from periodized athletic training, this idea proposes a solution to integrating the span boundary objective with other pre-training tasks like sentence order prediction. In strength conditioning, athletes cycle through focused training phases for different physical qualities, combining core compound lifts with accessory work in a structured schedule to achieve synergistic, non-interfering adaptations. Adapted to NLP pre-training, this involves a hierarchical objective scheduler where span masking serves as the continuous, foundational 'compound lift.' Secondary 'accessory' objectives are then introduced in rotating, scheduled phases or as a fixed auxiliary loss with a lower weighting. This method aims to combine the benefits of multi-task learning—forcing the model to develop general and specific linguistic skills—while mitigating the risk of objective interference and training instability, potentially leading to more robust and versatile language representations.

ideas.title: Edge-Scale SpanBERT for Question-Contaminated Evidence (QCE) Detection
ideas.description: This research develops a lightweight SpanBERT variant optimized for mobile and edge deployment, with a novel pre-training objective focused on 'Question-Contaminated Evidence' (QCE). Instead of generic span corruption, the model is trained on synthetic datasets where evidence documents are directly influenced by the phrasing of a potential question, teaching it to identify subtle, question-induced artifacts and biases within text spans. This enables efficient, on-device analysis of document-question pairs to flag manipulated or non-independent evidence, supporting applications in fact-checking assistants and secure document review without relying on cloud-based large models.

ideas.title: Proposition-Aware Semantic Alignment for Robust Question-Evidence Matching
ideas.description: This research develops a system to align questions with answer evidence by reasoning over their core semantic propositions rather than surface forms. It constructs explicit, canonical meaning representations for both inputs that abstract away from lexical choice and syntactic structure. The system then performs alignment using a neuro-symbolic reasoning module that identifies and executes the necessary inference steps to bridge logical and conceptual gaps between the question and the evidence. This approach ensures robustness to profound paraphrasing, changes in conceptual framing, and evidence that requires multi-fact integration to answer.

ideas.title: Adaptive Span Masking for Non-Text Sequences via Seismic Signal Processing Techniques
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from geophysics, specifically adaptive signal windowing techniques used in seismic data processing, to the problem of pre-training on non-text sequential data (DNA, protein, time-series). In seismology, analysts use statistical properties of the signal to dynamically define analysis windows that correspond to geologically meaningful events, improving denoising and interpretation. We propose applying this principle to create 'adaptive span masking' for self-supervised pre-training. Instead of masking random fixed-length spans, the masking strategy would be conditioned on the input sequence to target coherent functional or structural units—such as protein domains, exons, or seasonal cycles—identified by a lightweight auxiliary model or rule set. This approach, applied within a span prediction framework, should force the foundational model to develop a richer, more hierarchical understanding of the non-text data's inherent organization, potentially improving downstream task performance on tasks like function prediction, anomaly detection, or forecasting.

ideas.title: Semantic Homology Mapping for Question-Answer Alignment
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from bioinformatics, where the problem of identifying functionally similar genes across evolutionarily divergent species (despite significant sequence variability) parallels the NLP problem of aligning differently phrased questions with answer evidence. Bioinformatics solves this with homology detection tools and gene family databases that align sequences based on conserved functional domains. Adapted to this context, the idea is to develop 'semantic homology mapping' methods. These methods would treat questions and evidence passages as semantic sequences, learning to identify and align their core, answer-relevant functional roles or 'semantic domains.' This creates a robust alignment space that bridges lexical and syntactic variability by focusing on the underlying intent and informational purpose, enabling accurate question answering even when the surface forms differ significantly.

ideas.title: Ambiguity-Aware, Multi-Hypothesis Semantic Parsing for Robust Question Interpretation
ideas.description: This system first generates multiple candidate logical forms for a complex question using a probabilistic parser trained to capture semantic variation and underspecification. Each candidate is treated as a hypothesis and is used to initiate a parallel, constrained evidence search. A neuro-symbolic reasoner evaluates the coherence and evidential support for each interpretation against knowledge bases and text, dynamically pruning and re-ranking candidates. The final reasoning pathway is constructed using the highest-confidence interpretation, ensuring robustness to linguistic ambiguity and contextual nuance.

ideas.title: Structure-Aware Evidence Integration via Latent Reasoning Graph Induction
ideas.description: This research designs models that do not merely integrate cross-sentence information but explicitly infer and leverage the latent relational structure connecting evidence. A neural module first induces a soft, interpretable reasoning graph over the provided evidence sentences, labeling potential edges with discourse or logical relation types. This graph then acts as a dynamic attention guide for a subsequent reasoning module, which performs structured information aggregation along the graph's pathways. This approach ensures the model builds a globally coherent understanding of how disparate facts interconnect, significantly improving robustness on tasks requiring complex narrative or multi-step deductive reasoning.

ideas.title: Adaptive Span Masking via Progressive Overload
ideas.description: This idea adapts the principle of progressive overload from sports science to the problem of span masking in language model pre-training. In strength training, athletes systematically increase workout difficulty based on their current performance to optimize adaptation. Translated to the ML context, the model's own reconstruction loss on masked spans is used as a fitness signal to dynamically control the masking curriculum. An adaptive scheduler would gradually increase span length and masking density only when the model demonstrates proficiency at the current level, creating a self-paced, data-driven curriculum that aims to optimize learning efficiency and final model capability.

ideas.title: Distillation of Span Knowledge via Pruned Neural Architecture
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'canonical microcircuit' approach from computational neuroscience to create a highly efficient SpanBERT variant for edge devices. In neuroscience, the complex, redundant structure of a cortical column is distilled down to a minimal repeating circuit that preserves its core computational function. Analogously, we propose to analyze a full-scale SpanBERT to identify the minimal set of architectural components (e.g., specific attention patterns, layer interactions) that are fundamentally necessary for its span prediction performance. This distilled 'Canonical Span Circuit'—a compact, repeating module—becomes the blueprint for a new model architecture. This architecture is then pre-trained from the ground up using span masking objectives, resulting in a model that captures the essential span mechanisms of SpanBERT but within a footprint orders of magnitude smaller, making it suitable for mobile and edge deployment.

ideas.title: Adaptive Foraging for Evidence Retrieval in Open-Domain QA
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from behavioral ecology—specifically optimal foraging theory—to the open-domain QA problem where evidence is not pre-retrieved. In ecology, animals solve the problem of finding food in an unknown, patchy environment by dynamically balancing exploration and exploitation according to principles like the marginal value theorem. Adapted to QA, we propose developing an 'adaptive foraging' retrieval agent that treats the corpus as an information landscape. The system would not rely on pre-fetched evidence but would actively decide, based on real-time content analysis and predicted yield, whether to continue reading the current document/source, formulate a new query, or switch retrieval strategies, thereby optimizing the evidence-gathering process for accuracy and efficiency under the core constraint of no pre-provided evidence.

ideas.title: Provenance-Aware Reconciliation for Distant Supervision: Quantifying and Resolving Evidence Contradiction
ideas.description: This research develops a systematic framework to model the provenance and inherent reliability of documents used for distant supervision, moving beyond simple identification of noise. It introduces metrics to quantify the level of contradiction between evidence pieces from different sources and temporal contexts. The core of the approach is a reconciliation module that weights evidence based on provenance features and resolves conflicts to produce a confidence-scored, unified evidence set. This output is designed to directly improve the training of relation extraction or QA models by providing cleaner, traceable supervision signals.

ideas.title: Close the large human-machine performance gap via cognitive apprenticeship
ideas.description: Pursue research to raise model performance from 40% closer to the human ceiling of 80% by adapting a solution from cognitive psychology and educational science. The analogous problem—closing the gap between novice and expert performance—is solved through cognitive apprenticeship, where experts externalize their internal reasoning via think-aloud protocols and cognitive task analysis. The adapted idea is to implement a 'cognitive apprenticeship for AI' paradigm, training models on dense, process-oriented data of human problem-solving (including considered alternatives and self-corrections) rather than just final outcomes. This requires developing new methods for capturing human reasoning traces and architecting models capable of learning from these rich cognitive pathways to internalize more human-like problem-solving strategies.

ideas.title: Use a Chemical Reaction Network Model for Semantic Parsing and Reasoning
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from systems chemistry—the use of chemical reaction networks (CRNs) to model compositional, emergent systems—to the problem of semantic parsing for complex questions. In the analogous field, CRNs define basic molecular species and transformation rules to simulate how complex chemical systems arise from simpler interactions. Adapted to this research context, we treat fundamental semantic units as species and grammatical/logical operations as reaction rules. Translating a complex question into a logical form is thus modeled as searching for a pathway through this semantic reaction network. This approach provides a structured, computationally tractable framework to handle ambiguity, compositionality, and intermediate reasoning states, directly guiding the subsequent search for evidence and the execution of reasoning over that evidence.

ideas.title: Apply Ensemble Data Fusion Techniques from Remote Sensing to Multi-Document Evidence Synthesis
ideas.description: This idea adapts ensemble data fusion techniques from the field of remote sensing and geospatial analysis to the problem of synthesizing evidence from multiple documents. In remote sensing, solutions like pixel-level, feature-level, and decision-level fusion are used to integrate complementary data from disparate sensors into a unified, high-confidence model. Adapted to the research context, this involves creating a computational framework that treats each evidence document as a 'sensor' with specific biases and information types. The framework would implement a staged fusion process: extracting and aligning key entities and propositions across documents (analogous to feature-level fusion), applying reliability-weighted conflict resolution and temporal reasoning (analogous to decision-level fusion), to ultimately generate a robust, consolidated summary that accurately represents the synthesized evidence from all sources.

ideas.title: Auditing Evidential Representativeness for Robust Inference
ideas.description: This research develops a framework to diagnose when a set of retrieved evidence documents, though factually correct, presents a systematically biased or unrepresentative view that misleadingly supports a specific answer. The system simulates counterfactual evidence sets from a broader corpus or generative model to quantify the sensitivity of an answer to the sampling process of the evidence. The outcome is a formal measure of 'evidential fragility' that can flag high-risk inferences where conclusions are unduly dependent on the particular evidence slice presented, thereby challenging the independence of the conclusion from the evidence retrieval process itself.

ideas.title: Applying Radiocarbon Calibration to Distant Supervision Noise
ideas.description: This research adapts the calibration solution from radiocarbon dating to challenge the assumption that distantly gathered evidence provides high-quality supervision. In radiocarbon dating, raw measurements are calibrated against a known reference curve (e.g., tree-ring sequences) to correct for systematic noise and yield accurate dates. Analogously, we propose to treat labels generated through distant supervision as 'raw,' noisy measurements. The core idea is to construct a 'calibration model' by using a small, carefully annotated dataset as the reference standard. This model would learn the patterns of noise, bias, and contradiction inherent in the distant evidence sources. The output would be a framework that calibrates the distant supervision signals, transforming them into probabilistically reliable labels or providing uncertainty estimates, thereby mitigating the risks of training on unexamined distant evidence.

ideas.title: Multi-Source Synthesis as Geological Formation
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from historical geology, this idea challenges the single-answer extractive assumption in QA by modeling the answer process after geological synthesis. In stratigraphy, scientists combine data from multiple layers and sources to form a coherent historical narrative. Adapting this, the research proposes a model architecture that first identifies multiple evidentiary 'strata' within a text—separate facts, contexts, and qualifiers—and then performs an interpretive synthesis to generate a new text answer. This moves beyond extraction to constructive explanation, explicitly handling questions where the answer is a composite built from fragmented or distributed evidence in the source.

ideas.title: Question-Evidence Feedback Loop Detection via Instrumental Variable Analysis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a question and the evidence used to answer it are independent. Drawing an analogy from econometrics, it treats the question-evidence relationship as a potential endogeneity problem, where the phrasing of the question (the 'treatment') directly influences the content of the evidence (the 'outcome'), creating a feedback loop that biases results. The analogous solution is Instrumental Variable (IV) analysis, used to disentangle causal effects when variables are mutually influencing. Adapted to this context, the research would develop a framework to identify instrumental variables within the question-asking process—such as arbitrary lexical choices or preset query templates that should not affect evidence content under true independence. By applying IV-style statistical tests, the model can quantify the degree of influence the question phrasing exerts on the evidence, detect violations of independence, and potentially correct for this bias to retrieve more objective evidence.

ideas.title: Conflict-Aware Evidence Synthesis with Provenance and Contradiction Resolution
ideas.description: This system is designed to synthesize answers from multiple evidence documents while explicitly handling unreliable or conflicting information. It first analyzes each document for internal consistency and cross-document agreement, assigning a dynamic credibility score. A contradiction resolution module then identifies discrepancies and seeks corroborating evidence, favoring claims with higher credibility and support. The final output is a synthesized answer accompanied by a transparency report detailing the sources used, conflicts resolved, and the system's confidence in the provided information.
